title: Redirect Semantics, Method Rewriting, Credential Scope, and Loop Control · Automation and Data Client Systems · Changed-Input Lab
summary: A research archive team integrating an unfamiliar public dataset must satisfy changed endpoint, payload, authority, byte, and time constraints without a recipe for redirect semantics, method rewriting, credential scope, and loop control, producing auditable redirect policy engine. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of a Python 3.14 urllib and HTTP client layers auditable redirect policy engine with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through predicti…
objectives: Differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

Resolve Location references against the current request URL and validate the resulting destination each hop. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

Bound redirect hops, detect loops, retain the chain, and expose the policy decision to callers. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.python:
"""http-clients-python cumulative safe data-client portfolio.

Browser labs use deterministic request and response models only. Live urllib,
DNS, TLS, sockets, concurrency, and load require local Python 3.14 transfer gates.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RequestEvidence:
    method: str
    target: str
    attempt: int

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ResponseEvidence:
    status: int
    body_bytes: int
    valid: bool

print("Python HTTP client portfolio ready")

steps.title: Python Commit to the mission model · Lab Redirect Status Semantics
steps.instruction:
lab case — research archive client sends POST P-4 with authorization, then receives 302 ../moved, 307 https://upload.example.test/next, and a loop back to the first URL. Predict method, body, credential, origin, hop-budget, and SSRF decisions per step. Competency probe: Differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Python Commit to the mission model · Lab Redirect Status Semantics.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. demonstrate the result through python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports. For Python Commit to the mission model · Lab Redirect Status Semantics, keep Redirect Status Semantics connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

steps.title: Python Test an acceptance boundary · Lab Manual Redirect Defense
steps.instruction:
lab case — research archive client sends POST P-2 with authorization, then receives 302 ../moved, 307 https://upload.example.test/next, and a loop back to the first URL. Predict method, body, credential, origin, hop-budget, and SSRF decisions per step. One precondition fails while output still looks plausible. What is the correct response?
